summary: My current opinions on the technologies I work with as a Cloud Solution Architect. Every entry reflects production experience or active daily use — not blog reading. Updated quarterly.

entries.name: TypeScript
entries.summary: Default for any JS work. Type-driven design catches a class of bugs that no test ever will. This site is 100% TypeScript.
entries.useWhen: Anything beyond a 50-line script. Especially APIs, shared schemas, and React.
entries.avoidWhen: Tiny one-off automation where setup overhead outweighs the safety net.

entries.name: Python 3.11+
entries.summary: Go-to for AI workloads, data work, FastAPI services, and scripting. 3.11's faster interpreter is real in production.
entries.useWhen: AI / LLM apps, data pipelines, FastAPI backends, scripting.
entries.avoidWhen: CPU-bound hot paths or anywhere you need single-binary deploys.

entries.name: Bicep
entries.summary: Best DSL for Azure-only IaC. Cleaner than ARM, native deployment, no state file headaches. My preferred way to describe Azure resources.
entries.useWhen: Single-cloud Azure shops. Anything that lives entirely in ARM's resource model.
entries.avoidWhen: Multi-cloud, or when you need Terraform's cross-cloud state.

entries.name: ARM Templates
entries.summary: The bedrock of Azure IaC. Bicep compiles to ARM, and you still need to read raw ARM when debugging deployments. Deep familiarity is non-negotiable for Azure architects.
entries.useWhen: When Bicep tooling isn't available, debugging deployment failures, or consuming published Azure Quickstart templates.
entries.avoidWhen: Greenfield projects — write Bicep and let the compiler handle the JSON.

entries.name: Shell Scripts (Bash/Zsh)
entries.summary: The universal glue for automation, CI/CD pipelines, and Linux server management. Still the fastest path from idea to running script on any Unix-like system.
entries.useWhen: CI/CD automation, Linux server setup, quick glue between tools, Docker entrypoints.
entries.avoidWhen: Anything over ~200 lines — switch to Python at that point.

entries.name: Next.js App Router
entries.summary: Server components plus streaming have changed how I structure frontends. This site runs on it.
entries.useWhen: Marketing + dashboard hybrids, any SSR/SSG mix, anything needing edge rendering.
entries.avoidWhen: Pure SPAs where Vite is simpler, or extreme low-bundle requirements.

entries.name: FastAPI
entries.summary: Pydantic-typed APIs with auto-OpenAPI is the productivity peak for Python services.
entries.useWhen: Python backends, AI service wrappers, anything that needs a typed contract fast.
entries.avoidWhen: If your team is already on Django REST Framework and happy.

entries.name: Tailwind CSS v4
entries.summary: Oxide engine is fast, CSS-first config is sane. I no longer write component-level CSS.
entries.useWhen: Any new web project.
entries.avoidWhen: Design systems with strict token + theming requirements that need a CSS-in-JS lib.

entries.name: React Server Components
entries.summary: Real productivity wins for content-heavy sites once you internalize the server/client split.
entries.useWhen: Content + dashboard apps, anywhere data fetching dominates render.

entries.name: Azure Container Apps
entries.summary: My default for stateless services. KEDA, revisions, managed identity, and Dapr — all built in. Saves weeks of Kubernetes config.
entries.useWhen: HTTP APIs, event-driven workers, anything that doesn't need full AKS knobs.
entries.avoidWhen: DaemonSets, custom CNI, or sub-100ms cold start requirements.

entries.name: Azure Functions (Flex Consumption)
entries.summary: The cold-start story is finally good. VNet integration on Consumption is the real unlock for serverless in regulated environments.
entries.useWhen: Event triggers, glue code, scheduled jobs, anything spiky.
entries.avoidWhen: Long-running workloads or when Container Apps' richer model fits better.

entries.name: Docker
entries.summary: The unit of deployment for everything I ship. Reproducible builds, portable environments, and the foundation for Kubernetes and ACA.
entries.useWhen: Any application that needs consistent environments across dev, CI, and prod.
entries.avoidWhen: Tiny scripts where a virtual environment or pip install is sufficient.

entries.name: Linux
entries.summary: The operating system for everything in production. Whether it's App Service on Linux, AKS nodes, or a VM, Linux is the default.
entries.useWhen: All cloud workloads. Default for servers, containers, and VMs.
entries.avoidWhen: Windows-specific workloads like .NET Framework or legacy IIS apps.

entries.name: Microsoft AI Foundry
entries.summary: Hosted agents + evals + prompt optimizer is the most cohesive AI platform on Azure right now.
entries.useWhen: Production agents, RAG systems where you want managed evals.
entries.avoidWhen: Tiny prototypes — overhead vs. just hitting the SDK directly.

entries.name: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
entries.summary: Still the right answer when Container Apps' constraints bite. AKS Automatic narrows the ops gap significantly.
entries.useWhen: Multi-tenant platforms, GPU workloads, custom networking, sidecar-heavy apps.
entries.avoidWhen: Small teams without dedicated platform engineers.

entries.name: Azure AI Search
entries.summary: Hybrid + semantic ranker has become the default RAG retrieval layer in my designs.
entries.useWhen: Production RAG, enterprise search, anywhere you need vector + keyword fusion.
entries.avoidWhen: Toy projects — pgvector or Qdrant are cheaper to start with.

entries.name: Azure Cosmos DB (NoSQL)
entries.summary: Global distribution + vector index is genuinely useful. Pricing still demands careful partition design.
entries.useWhen: Global, multi-region writes; document workloads that need low-latency reads everywhere.
entries.avoidWhen: Predictable single-region OLTP — Postgres Flex is half the cost.

entries.name: Azure Postgres Flexible Server
entries.summary: Default relational store. pgvector + read replicas + private endpoints solves 80% of needs.
entries.useWhen: Most apps with relational data, OLTP, RAG with pgvector at small/medium scale.
entries.avoidWhen: Truly global multi-region writes — that's Cosmos territory.

entries.name: Azure Service Bus
entries.summary: The boring, correct answer for enterprise messaging. Sessions + dead-letter + dup-detect are gold.
entries.useWhen: Order processing, command queues, anything needing FIFO + transactions.
entries.avoidWhen: High-throughput telemetry — that's Event Hubs.

entries.name: Azure Event Hubs
entries.summary: Kafka-compatible without operating Kafka. Default ingress for telemetry and event-driven pipelines.
entries.useWhen: Log ingestion, IoT, high-throughput event streaming.

entries.name: Azure Cache for Redis
entries.summary: Indispensable for session, rate-limiting, and Celery brokers. Enterprise tier when you need geo-rep.

entries.name: Azure Key Vault
entries.summary: Pair with managed identity and you can almost forget secrets exist. The baseline for any secure Azure workload.
entries.useWhen: Always — it's the baseline.

entries.name: AWS
entries.summary: Solid experience with core services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS). I work primarily in Azure but stay current on AWS for multi-cloud and advisory work.
entries.useWhen: Multi-cloud architectures, migrations from AWS to Azure, or teams already AWS-invested.
entries.avoidWhen: Azure-first shops — switching costs are high.

entries.name: Alibaba Cloud
entries.summary: Hands-on experience from global project work. Relevant for APAC deployments and China market requirements.
entries.useWhen: China market or APAC-specific compliance requirements.
entries.avoidWhen: General global workloads where Azure or AWS are the standard.

entries.name: GitHub Actions
entries.summary: OIDC to Azure made this the obvious choice. Reusable workflows + matrix builds cover 95% of pipelines. This site's CI/CD runs on it.
entries.useWhen: Any CI/CD that targets Azure or GitHub-hosted code.
entries.avoidWhen: Heavy self-hosted needs where Azure DevOps agent pools are still better.

entries.name: Azure Developer CLI (azd)
entries.summary: `azd up` is the right level of abstraction for app teams who want to skip infra ceremony.
entries.useWhen: Greenfield Azure apps, hackathons, app-team self-service.
entries.avoidWhen: Platform teams who need full Bicep / Terraform control.

entries.name: Claude Code
entries.summary: Daily driver for everything except trivial edits. Skill files + persistent memory are the productivity multiplier.
entries.useWhen: Multi-step coding, research, refactors, architecture work.
entries.avoidWhen: 30-second snippet edits — autocomplete still wins on latency.

entries.name: GitHub Copilot
entries.summary: Inline completion is a baseline. Agent mode is closing the gap with Claude Code fast.
entries.useWhen: Always on for daily coding.

entries.name: Playwright
entries.summary: End of the conversation about E2E testing. Trace viewer alone justifies it.
entries.useWhen: Any E2E or cross-browser testing.
entries.avoidWhen: Pure unit tests.

entries.name: Terraform
entries.summary: Still the lingua franca for multi-cloud and platform teams. AzureRM provider has mostly caught up to Bicep.
entries.useWhen: Multi-cloud, regulated shops, large platform teams with existing Terraform investment.
entries.avoidWhen: Single-cloud Azure with no Terraform tax already paid.

entries.name: Azure CLI (az)
entries.summary: Still the most reliable way to script Azure. JMESPath queries are underrated.

entries.name: Vagrant
entries.summary: Reproducible dev VMs with a one-file spec. Useful for testing IaC and Linux configs before cloud deployment.
entries.useWhen: Local VM-based dev environments, testing Ansible/shell provisioning scripts, or replicating on-prem setups.
entries.avoidWhen: Container-first workflows where Docker Compose gives you the same reproducibility faster.

entries.name: OpenAI Codex / o3
entries.summary: Strong coding reasoning capabilities. Using in agentic pipelines for code generation and automated review tasks.
entries.useWhen: Agentic coding workflows, code review automation, complex reasoning-heavy code tasks.
entries.avoidWhen: Latency-sensitive flows where Claude Code's sub-second response matters more.

entries.name: Azure Portal
entries.summary: For exploration and breakglass only. Everything that ships should be code.

entries.name: MCP Servers
entries.summary: The emerging standard for tool surfaces in agentic workflows. Already shipping in my agent pipelines.
entries.useWhen: Exposing internal tools to Claude / Copilot / Cursor.

entries.name: Generative AI & Agentic AI
entries.summary: Not a technology but a practice shift. Designing systems around LLM capabilities — prompt engineering, agent orchestration, and output validation — is now a core skill.
entries.useWhen: AI-powered products, intelligent automation, RAG systems, copilot-style features.
entries.avoidWhen: Deterministic problems with no ambiguity — regex and rules are still faster and cheaper.

entries.name: RAG with Hybrid Retrieval
entries.summary: Keyword + vector + semantic ranker is the default. Pure-vector RAG is a 2023 mistake.
entries.useWhen: Any production RAG over heterogeneous content.

entries.name: Continuous AI Evals
entries.summary: If you're shipping agents without evals you're flying blind. Foundry / Promptflow make this trivial now.
entries.useWhen: Always when an LLM is in the request path.

entries.name: Managed Identity Everywhere
entries.summary: Connection strings are a smell. System or user-assigned MI + RBAC is the only acceptable pattern for greenfield Azure.
entries.useWhen: Always, no exceptions for greenfield Azure apps.

entries.name: Zero Trust Networking
entries.summary: Private endpoints + NSGs + Entra Conditional Access. The new baseline for anything touching prod data.

entries.name: Azure Well-Architected Framework
entries.summary: The structured lens I use to review and design Azure architectures. Reliability, Security, Cost, Operational Excellence, and Performance — applying all five pillars changes how you architect.
entries.useWhen: Architecture reviews, greenfield designs, customer workshops.

entries.name: Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework
entries.summary: End-to-end guidance for Azure migrations and cloud strategy. CAF's landing zones and governance tracks save months of re-inventing patterns.
entries.useWhen: Enterprise Azure migrations, landing zone design, governance setup.

entries.name: Serverless Architecture
entries.summary: Functions + event-driven design eliminates most infrastructure toil for the right class of problems. Flex Consumption has solved the cold-start problem.
entries.useWhen: Spiky workloads, event processing, API backends with unpredictable load.
entries.avoidWhen: Long-running, stateful, or latency-sensitive workloads.

entries.name: Hybrid Cloud Solutions
entries.summary: On-prem to cloud is rarely all-or-nothing. Azure Arc, ExpressRoute, and hybrid identity patterns are regular tools in my architecture kit.
entries.useWhen: Regulated industries, data sovereignty requirements, gradual migration scenarios.

entries.name: Treat IaC as a Product
entries.summary: Versioned modules, semantic releases, what-if as a CI gate. Stop copy-pasting Bicep snippets.

entries.name: FinOps Tagging Discipline
entries.summary: Boring, but cost-by-tag is the only scalable way to attribute cloud spend. Enforce via Azure Policy.

entries.name: Agentic Coding Workflows
entries.summary: Plan → execute → verify loops with skill files reliably outperform single-shot prompting on real tasks.

entries.name: OpenAI Voice Live API
entries.summary: I put OpenAI Voice Live API in Adopt for production voice agents because it removes coordination complexity — end-to-end real-time voice collapses STT, LLM orchestration, and TTS into one integrated interaction path. Biggest win: fewer synchronization bugs and lower latency. Biggest trade-off: cost at scale.
entries.useWhen: When you need an end-to-end voice agent without separate STT and TTS — natural conversation quality matters and you want interruption handling, turn detection, and function calling in one integrated path.
entries.avoidWhen: When you are very cost constrained, only need transcription, or your backends are slow and unreliable. A stitched STT + LLM + TTS pipeline is cheaper for high-volume or purely transactional bots.
